questions.prompt: An amplifier has A = 1000 and D = 11. If A changes by 10%, by how much does A_f change?
questions.explanation: Fractional change in A_f = (1/D) × fractional change in A = (1/11) × 10% ≈ 0.91%. This is the gain desensitization property of feedback.

questions.prompt: Negative feedback reduces nonlinear distortion by a factor of:
questions.explanation: If the open-loop amplifier generates distortion voltage V_d, the closed-loop distortion is V_d/D = V_d/(1+Aβ). Same desensitivity factor D divides all imperfections.

questions.prompt: For series mixing at the input of a feedback amplifier, the input impedance R_if is:
questions.explanation: Series mixing (voltage subtraction) at the input increases input impedance: R_if = R_i × D. A higher input impedance means less loading on the source — closer to the ideal voltage amplifier.

questions.prompt: For shunt sampling at the output of a feedback amplifier, the output impedance R_of is:
questions.explanation: Shunt sampling (sensing voltage) at the output decreases output impedance: R_of = R_o / D. A lower output impedance means better voltage delivery to the load — again, closer to the ideal.

questions.prompt: The gain-bandwidth product of a feedback amplifier (compared to the open-loop amplifier) is:
questions.explanation: Feedback trades gain for bandwidth. A_f = A/D but BW_f = BW × D. The product A_f × BW_f = (A/D)(BW × D) = A × BW. Gain-bandwidth product is preserved.

questions.prompt: If an open-loop amplifier has bandwidth 10 kHz and D = 20, what is the closed-loop bandwidth?
questions.explanation: BW_f = BW × D = 10 kHz × 20 = 200 kHz. Feedback extends the bandwidth by the same factor D that it reduces the gain.

questions.prompt: Why does negative feedback reduce noise that enters the amplifier at the input?
options: The feedback network filters out noise frequencies
Noise at input is treated the same as the input signal — it is reduced by D
Noise is only reduced if it enters at the output
questions.correctAnswer: Noise at input is treated the same as the input signal — it is reduced by D
questions.explanation: Noise appearing at the input is treated identically to the input signal — it passes through the same closed-loop gain A_f = A/D. So input-referred noise is reduced by D relative to the open-loop case.

questions.prompt: What is the key reason that feedback can stabilize gain against transistor β (h_FE) variations?
options: Feedback changes the transistor's operating point
The closed-loop gain depends mainly on β (resistors), not on transistor parameters
Transistor h_FE becomes constant with feedback
questions.correctAnswer: The closed-loop gain depends mainly on β (resistors), not on transistor parameters
questions.explanation: When Aβ >> 1, A_f ≈ 1/β. The feedback network β is made from stable passive resistors. So A_f no longer depends on the transistor's h_FE — even if h_FE varies from 50 to 300, A_f stays nearly constant.

questions.prompt: For shunt mixing at the input (current subtraction), the input impedance R_if is:
questions.explanation: Shunt mixing at the input decreases input impedance: R_if = R_i / D. For a current amplifier, low input impedance is ideal — feedback moves it toward ideal.

questions.prompt: An amplifier has open-loop gain A = 500, D = 51. An open-loop distortion of 100 mV is produced. What is the closed-loop distortion?
questions.explanation: Closed-loop distortion = V_d / D = 100 mV / 51 ≈ 1.96 mV. The distortion is divided by the same factor D = 1 + Aβ.

questions.prompt: For series sampling at the output (sensing output current), the output impedance R_of is:
questions.explanation: Series sampling (sensing current) at the output increases output impedance: R_of = R_o × D. For a current amplifier or transconductance amplifier, high output impedance is ideal.

questions.prompt: What are the six key properties of negative feedback (in order)?
options: Gain × D, distortion × D, noise × D, R_i × D, R_o × D, BW × D
Gain ÷ D, distortion ÷ D, noise ÷ D, impedances change by D, BW × D
Gain ÷ D, distortion ÷ D, noise unchanged, R_i × D, R_o ÷ D, BW × D
questions.correctAnswer: Gain ÷ D, distortion ÷ D, noise ÷ D, impedances change by D, BW × D
questions.explanation: Negative feedback: (1) A_f = A/D (gain reduced), (2) distortion ÷ D, (3) input-referred noise ÷ D, (4) impedances change by D or 1/D depending on topology, (5) bandwidth × D. One cost (gain), five benefits.
